evidence_excerpt: He died three months after his final admission and six months after the onset of his illness; the tumor proved to be lethal within six months despite radiotherapy.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Persistent weight loss and anorexia leading to readmission, dehydration and inanition, increasing lethargy, and complications including gastric perforation, diabetes, pneumonia, and wound dehiscence.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: A ventriculoperitoneal shunt procedure was carried out.
He was readmitted in September 1980, because of weight loss and anorexia.
Readmission due to weight loss and anorexia indicated clinical decline.
A shunt revision resulted in some improvement in his mental status and a gastrostomy was done to improve his nutrition.
Shunt revision resulted in some improvement in his mental status.
In the ensuing weeks he became increasingly lethargic.
His course was complicated by gastric perforation, diabetes, pneumonia and wound dehiscence.
He died three months after his final admission and six months after the onset of his illness.

notes_on_uncertainty: Definitive tissue diagnosis (pineocytoma) was established at autopsy; pre-treatment diagnosis was based on imaging and clinical course. The chosen reference time is the initial hospital admission related to the condition. Follow-up lasted only about six months, so 'long-term' quality of life is inferred from the late course prior to death rather than beyond 12 months.
